region_of_origin: Asia (widely cultivated across East Asia, South Asia, and Southeast Asia)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 covers turnip as a food entity; the specific preparation state (raw, peeled) is not separately coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226734001 refers to turnip as a substance; raw peeled preparation state is not separately encoded.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw peeled white turnip is approximately 130 g, equivalent to one medium-sized turnip, providing roughly 29 kcal and 4.4 g of carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: White turnip, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for white turnip, raw, peeled (cr_id F14040063). All core macros and micronutrients sourced from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several vitamins not reported by HPB; these are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 11564 (Turnips, raw) where available, and set to -1 where neither source reports the value.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for raw peeled white turnip was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 62 is derived from the published GI value for turnip reported in Atkinson et al. (2008), Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283, which lists turnip at GI 62 (glucose reference). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (62 × 4.42) / 100 ≈ 2.7, using 130 g serving size and 3.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (24 mg/100 g) and high potassium (320 mg/100 g) yielding a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio consistent with DASH dietary principles
hypertension.guidance: Raw peeled white turnip is an excellent choice for individuals with hypertension; its very low sodium content and high potassium level actively support blood pressure regulation, but pickled or salted turnip preparations must be avoided as they can contain several hundred milligrams of sodium per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (3.4 g/100 g), moderate GI of 62 yielding a very low glycemic load of 2.7 per 130 g serving, and dietary fibre (1.7 g/100 g) supporting glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: White turnip is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low glycemic load per serving; it can be consumed raw or lightly cooked, but sweetened or pickled preparations that add sugar and sodium should be avoided to preserve its glycaemic benefits.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat (0.0 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, low sodium, high potassium, and dietary fibre supporting cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw peeled white turnip is a heart-healthy food with negligible fat, zero cholesterol, and a high potassium-to-sodium ratio; regular inclusion in the diet supports vascular health and complements a cardioprotective dietary pattern, provided it is not consumed in high-sodium pickled forms.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Cruciferous vegetable with sulforaphane precursors and vitamin C (~21 mg/100 g) providing anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: White turnip may be beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory phytochemicals and vitamin C content; pickled preparations high in sodium should be avoided as excess sodium may worsen tissue swelling, and individuals with cruciferous vegetable sensitivity should monitor their individual response.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White turnip (raw, peeled) is a root vegetable belonging to the Brassicaceae (cruciferous) family, widely cultivated and consumed across East Asia, South Asia, Southeast Asia, and the Mediterranean. The raw peeled form presents as a firm, crisp, white-fleshed root with a mildly peppery and slightly sweet taste. It is commonly eaten raw in salads, julienned as a garnish, or used as an ingredient in soups, stews, stir-fries, and braised dishes. In Singapore and Chinese culinary traditions, white turnip (known as 白萝卜, bai luobo or lo bak) is a staple ingredient in dishes such as chai tow kway (carrot cake), braised pork with radish, and clear soups. Nutritionally, raw peeled white turnip is very low in calories (22 kcal/100 g) and carbohydrates (3.4 g/100 g), with a moderate glycemic index of 62 but a very low glycemic load of approximately 2.7 per 130 g serving due to its high water content (~91.9 g/100 g). It provides 1.3 g protein, negligible fat, 1.7 g dietary fibre, and notable potassium (320 mg/100 g) with low sodium (24 mg/100 g), making it highly suitable for hypertension and cardiovascular health management. Its vitamin C content (~21 mg/100 g) and sulforaphane precursors contribute anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties relevant to rheumatoid arthritis and general immune support. The food is naturally vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, and is appropriate for low-calorie, low-sodium, and plant-based dietary patterns.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14040063) at confidence 0.95. Secondary micronutrients (B vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 11564 (Turnips, raw). GI value sourced from Atkinson et al. (2008) peer-reviewed publication via the University of Sydney GI Database framework. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14040063); white turnip is a non-oily root vegetable that naturally contains negligible fat. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are all confirmed zero or negligible per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 11564 (Turnips, raw), consistent with the naturally fat-free profile of this vegetable. cholesterol_mg is naturally zero as this is a plant-based food. vitamin_b12_mcg is naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived components. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Turnips, raw); plant-based root vegetables do not synthesise vitamin D. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is set to -1 as HPB does not report this value and USDA SR Legacy reports a negligible RAE value that is not reliably confirmed for the peeled form; follow-up with HPB or USDA FoodData Central recommended. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. (2008), Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283, as no direct GI measurement for raw peeled white turnip was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Note: white turnip (Brassica rapa) and daikon/Chinese radish (Raphanus sativus) are distinct vegetables sometimes confused in culinary contexts; this record refers specifically to white turnip (Brassica rapa subsp. rapa). The 'keto' dietary tag from the input has been retained in dietary_flags context given the very low net carbohydrate content (1.7 g net carbs per 100 g after fibre subtraction).
